The steel corrosion loss ratios ranged from 0% to 15.1%, and the axial load ratios varied from 0.15 to 0.9. Based on the test results, degradation models for loading capacity, stiffness, ductility and energy dissipation capacity of columns are proposed. 2. Experimental program 2.1. Specimen details In order to investigate the inﬂuence of rebar corrosion on seismic behavior of circular reinforced concrete columns, a series of tests of corroded reinforced concrete columns with different corrosion levels under combined reversed cyclic displacement excursions and axial loads were conducted. Thirteen circular rein- forced concrete columns with identical dimensions and reinforcement layout were tested. The dimensions and reinforcement layout are shown in Fig. 1 . Each speci- men consisted of a 260 mm diameter and 1000 mm long column cast integrally with a 1300 mm /C2 360 mm /C2 400 mm stub. The clear concrete cover to the spirals was 30 mm. The column represents the part of a bridge pier or a building column between the section of maximum moment and the point of contraﬂexure. The stub represents a discontinuity, such as a beam column joint or a footing. These columns had an effective length of 820 mm and an span-to-depth ratio ( L/D) of 3.15. Six 150 mm /C2 150 mm /C2 150 mm cubes were cast along with the specimens, and the 28-day mean cube compressive strength was 32.4 MPa. Six 16 mm diame- ter steel bars were used as longitudinal reinforcement, and 8 mm diameter steel bars with a spacing of 100 mm were used as stirrups. For the steel reinforcement, tensile tests were carried out and it was determined that for the 16 mm and 8 mm bars, the yield strength f y were 373.2 MPa and 327 MPa, respectively, and the tensile strength fu were 572.3 MPa and 510.7 MPa, respectively. The controlled variables for this study were corrosion level and axial load ratio which is deﬁned as the ratio of axial load (i.e. compressive axial load in the direction parallel to the col- umn axis) to the product of concrete compressive strength and section area i.e. n ¼ N=ðf 0 c AÞ. The specimens were designated as Cx–y, in which C standing for circu- lar cross section of specimens, x standing for the integer part of rebar corrosion loss ratio (the mass loss ratio of the rebar, %), and y standing for the decimal part of axial load ratio. For example, C9–15 stands for the circular reinforced concrete column with the rebar corrosion loss ratio of 9.5%, and the axial load ratio of 0.15. Details of specimens are summarized in Table 1 . The tests of column specimens C0–15, C0–25, C0–40, C9–15, C4–25, C9–25, C9–40 and C14–32 were carried out by Li and Gong at Dalian University of Technology [14]. 2.2. Accelerated corrosion An external current method [15] was utilized to induce corrosion in the speci- mens. The specimens were immersed in a water tank containing 3.5% NaCl solution. The reinforcement cage of each specimen was used as the anode and the stainless plate immersed in the tank was used as cathode, through which a constant current was applied. In the corrosion test, specimens were corroded at the expected corro- sion level deﬁned by the mass loss. This predeﬁned mass loss is estimated by using Faraday’s law, i.e. m t ¼ð t /C2 I /C2 55:847Þ=ð2 /C2 96487Þ, where t is the duration and I is the avera

Behavior of corrosion damaged circular reinforced concrete columns under cyclic loading Ying Ma a,b,⇑, Yi Che a, Jinxin Gong a a State Key Laboratory of Coastal and Offshore Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian City, Liaoning Province 116024, China b School of Civil Engineering and Communication, North China University of Water Resources and Electric Power, Zhengzhou 450011, China article info Article history: Received 25 April 2011 Received in revised form 15 October 2011 Accepted 9 November 2011 Available online 19 December 2011 Keywords: Reinforced concrete Circular column Corrosion Axial load Seismic behavior Cyclic loading Ductility Energy dissipation abstract This paper presents the results of an investigation on degradation of seismic performance of reinforced concrete (RC) columns damaged by reinforcement corrosion. A total of 13 reinforced concrete circular columns were tested under combined cyclic lateral displacement excursions and constant axial load after being subjected to accelerated corrosion tests. The controlled variables in the test were corrosion level and axial load ratio. The steel corrosion loss ratios ranged from 0% to 15.1%, and the axial load ratios var- ied from 0.15 to 0.9. Based on the test results, degradation models for loading capacity, stiffness, ductility and energy dissipation capacity of columns were proposed. Test results show that higher corrosion levels and higher axial loads result in less stable hysteretic loops with more severe strength and stiffness degra- dations and worse ductility, and that the energy dissipation of corroded specimens at the same displace- ment excursions is approximately the same as that of control specimens, regardless of the corrosion level, when the rebar corrosion loss ratio is less than 14%. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Corrosion of steel bars is one of the major sources of durability degradation of reinforced concrete structures that are exposed to a chloride-bearing environment [1–7]. It may also adversely affect the structural behavior such as reduction of loading capacity of members due to steel area loss, deterioration of bond between steel bar and concrete and anchorage of steel bar. In particular transverse reinforcement is more vulnerable to corrosion due to its smaller diameter and being closer to the concrete surface, and thus leads to deterioration of conﬁnement provided by transverse reinforcement. Moreover, a large number of reinforced concrete structures that suffer from high level corrosion attack are located in high seismic risk regions and the structural behavior of these structures are of great concern. Therefore, it is essential to clarify the inﬂuence of rebar corrosion on the seismic behavior of rein- forced concrete members. Behavior of corroded RC columns under cyclic loading has been investigated by many researchers. However, these investigations were mainly focused on columns with rectangular cross section. Lee et al. [8] carried out cyclic loading tests on six rectangular RC columns corroded at different levels, and found that the deteriora- tion of structural behavior was mainly caused by the decline in the conﬁning effect, primarily due to the spalling of concrete cover and the reduction of mechanical properties of the corroded rebar. Bou- sias et al. [9] tested rectangular reinforced concrete columns to investigate the effect of some important parameters, such as the number of ﬁber-reinforced

The corroded specimens C9–15, C4–25, C9–25, C9–40 and C14–32, of which the axial load ratios were less than 0.5, failed in a similar manner. The corrosion cracks in these specimens widened as the lateral load increased and was followed by the formation of transverse ﬂexural cracks on the tension side. The ﬂexural cracking loads of these corroded specimens, at which the ﬂexural cracks were ﬁrst observed, were much higher than those of the corresponding control specimens C0–15, C0–25 and C0–40, respectively. No new ﬂexural cracks were observed after yielding of longitudinal reinforcement in these corroded specimens. These corroded specimens failed in brittle manners. The crack spacing at failure in corroded specimens were greater than those in the cor- responding control specimens. Fig. 4 b displays the failure pattern of specimen C9–15. In the corroded specimens with axial load ratio greater than 0.5 (specimens C11–60, C11–75, C13–75, C12–90 and C15–90), trans- verse ﬂexural cracks were ﬁrst observed. As lateral force increased, new ﬂexural cracks occurred and developed diagonally. After yield- ing of longitudinal reinforcement, no new cracks were observed except that original longitudinal cracks induced by corrosion wid- ened and developed upwardly or downwardly. As displacement was increased, spalling of cover concrete occurred at the column end and was followed by buckling of longitudinal bars and crush- ing of core concrete in the subsequent. Fig. 4 c displays the crack pattern and spalling of cover concrete of specimen C15–90 at failure. Note that specimen C14–32 failed in a more brittle manner due to its loss of entire area of steel bars caused by corrosion. The fail- ure pattern of specimen C14–32 is shown in Fig. 4 d. 4. Discussions 4.1. Hysteretic loops The inﬂuences of rebar corrosion and axial load on hysteretic loops, ductility, and energy dissipation capacity of columns are investigated here based on test results. Lateral force–displacement hysteretic loops for each specimen are shown in Fig. 5 . It should be noted that energy dissipation capacity and ductility of specimens decrease with the increase of rebar corrosion level and axial load ratio. Specimens C0–25, Fig. 2. C4–25 and C9–25 had the same axial load ratio, however less sta- ble hysteretic loops with smaller hysteretic loop area were found in severely corrosion-damaged specimen C9–25. On the other hand, specimens C9–15, C9–25 and C9–40 had the same corrosion level, poor hysteretic responses were measured in the specimens subjected to higher axial loads. Compared with axial load, the corrosion level probably is a more dominating factor that affects the hysteretic behavior of specimens. This phenomenon can be clearly seen by the compari- son of hysteretic loops of specimens C9–40 and C14–32 as shown in Fig. 5g and h, respectively. Specimen C14–32 that was subjected to lower axial load with more severe corrosion damage exhibited poor energy dissipation ability and failed in a brittle manner. High- er axial loads may lead to further reduction of energy dissipation ability and ductility of the corroded specimens. In specimens C11–75, C13–75, C12–90 and C15–90 that were subjected to se- vere rebar corrosion and high axial loads, poor hysteretic responses were observed. Fig. 5. Lateral force–displacement response. Y. Ma et al. / Construction and Building Materials 29 (2012) 548–556 551

4.2. Envelope curve The envelop curves of the lateral force–displacement response of all specimens that are obtained by averaging the maximum absolute values in positive and negative directions for each cycle of hysteretic loops are shown in Fig. 6. It is illustrated that for slightly corroded columns subjected to lower axial loads, minor reduction of loading capacity and stiffness is observed compared with the non-corroded control columns, while for severely corroded columns subjected to higher axial loads, signiﬁcant degradation of loading capacity is found in the descending branch of the curve. The typical load–displacement curve of a specimen subjected to reversed lateral load combined with axial load is displayed in Fig. 7 , where F y and Dy are the yield load and corresponding dis- placement that are determined based on the assumption that the area of hatched region SOAB equals to that of hatched region SYNB, respectively; Fu is the ultimate load, which is deﬁned as 85% the maximum lateral load and Du is the corresponding ultimate displacement. The experimental results for all specimens are sum- marized in Table 2 . For specimens C0–25 and C9–25 that had the same axial load ratio of 0.25, the yield load of the corroded speci- men C9–25 was reduced by 8.52% that of the control specimen C0–25; while the ultimate load of specimen C9–25 was reduced by 8.8%. For specimens C0–40 and C14–32 with approximately equal axial load ratio, the yield load and the ultimate load of spec- imen C14–32 was reduced by 18.28% and 10.83% those of specimen C0–40, respectively. Thus, it can be concluded that higher corro- sion level and higher axial load ratio result in lower loading capacity. The ratios of yield load of the corroded specimens to that of the control specimens under different axial load ratios, n, are shown in Fig. 8. Similarly, the ultimate load ratios are plotted in Fig. 9. Based on linear regression of test data from this study and Ref. [11], the yield load of corroded columns can be expressed in terms of corro- sion loss ratio q and yield load of non-corroded control columns Fy0. Fy ¼ Fy0ð1 /C0 0:885qÞð 1Þ In a similar way, the ultimate load of corroded columns Fu can be related to the corrosion loss ratio q and ultimate load of non- corroded control columns Fu0 as following. Fu ¼ Fu0ð1 /C0 0:878qÞð 2Þ 4.3. Stiffness degradation For every three cycles under the same displacement, the stiff- ness for specimens is deﬁned as [16] KD ¼ Kþ D þ K/C0 D 2 ð3Þ Kþ D ¼ X3 j¼1 Fþ j;max= X3 j¼1 Dþ j ; K/C0 D ¼ X3 j¼1 F/C0 j;max= X3 j¼1 D/C0 j ð4Þ where Fj,max and Dj are the maximum lateral load within a cycle and its corresponding displacement, respectively. For comparison, the calculated stiffness for each displacement level is normalized with respect to the stiffness of the ﬁrst dis- placement level. The normalized stiffness, g, versus the displace- ment for the specimens is shown in Fig. 10 . Note that the stiffness of the corroded specimens, compared with the control specimens, declines rapidly. The stiffness degradation of the corroded specimens during the whole loading process can also be expressed as the ratios of aver- age normalized stiffness of corroded columns to that of non-cor- roded control columns, g=g0: The average normalized stiffness, g; is the average value of normalized stiffness g at each displacement level. Fig. 11 shows g=g0 of specimens with different axial load ra- tios varying with the corrosion loss ratio q. As shown the axial load ratio of specimens has minor inﬂuence on g=g0. Therefore, the rela- tion between g=g0 and corrosion loss ratio q can be obtained by the linear regression of test data. The relation is as follows g ¼ g0ð1 /C0 1:182qÞð 5Þ 4.4. Ductility and energy dissipation To quantify the response of columns, it is desirable to deﬁne re- sponse indices that quantitatively describe the columns’ behavior. parameters and by energy dissipation capacity. Both types of indi- cators are computed in this paper to investigate the seismic behav- ior of columns. The displacement ductility factor is deﬁned as l ¼ Du=Dy ð6Þ where Du is the ultimate displacement; and Dy is the yield displacement. The ultimate rotation can be computed as h ¼ Du=L ð7Þ where L is the distance from the stub interface to the loading point. The calculated l and h for each column are listed in Table 2 . As shown in Table 2, the none-corroded control columns exhibit good ductility with ductility factor l ranging between 5.82 and 8.46. However, for specimens subjected to high level of corrosion, ductility factor reduces remarkably with the increase of axial load. For lightly corroded columns with the corrosion loss ratio of rebar less than 5%, the decreases in loading capacity and ductility are negligible small; while for columns with the corrosion loss ratio more than 5%, the decreases in loading capacity and ductility are signiﬁcant. The ratios of the displacement ductility factor of the corroded column and the corresponding non-corroded control col- umn, l/l0, are calculated based on test data, and are plotted in Fig. 12 as a function of corrosion loss ratio q. The relation of l/l0 ratio and q obtained by linear regression of test data from this study and Ref. [11] is l ¼ l0ð1 /C0 1:795qÞð 8Þ The cumulative energy dissipation during the test until failure can be calculated as Esum ¼ XN i¼1 Ei ¼ XN i¼1 XM j¼1 Eij ð9Þ where Ei is the energy dissipation for ith cycle calculated by the hatched area SABCDEA as shown in Fig. 13; N is the number of cycles until failure; and M is the number of divided bars displayed in Fig. 13 . For comparison purposes, it is convenient to normalize the dissipated energy by kc ¼ Esum mEy ; Ey ¼ 1 2 FyDy ð10Þ where kc is the normalized dissipated energy, which represents the dissipated energy after yielding of longitudinal reinforcement; m is the number of cycles from yielding to failure; and Fy is the yield load. Moreover, equivalent viscous damping he, as another energy dissipation parameter, was determined by the ratio of measured cyclic area ( SABCDEA) to idealized elastoplastic area ( SDOBM + SDODN) as shown in Fig. 13. The expression for equivalent viscous damping is he ¼ 1 2p /C2 SABCDEA SDOBM þ SDODN ð11Þ By using Eqs. (9)–(11), the energy dissipation, normalized dissi- pated energy and equivalent viscous damping of each specimen are calculated and the results are listed in Table 2. As shown, the en- ergy dissipation decreases as increasing axial load and corrosion. A comparison was made among specimens with axial load ratio of 0.25 (specimens C4–25, C9–25 and C0–25). The cumulative energy dissipation of slightly corroded specimen C4–25 was 98% that of the control column C0–25; while for moderately corroded specimen C9–25, the cumulative energy dissipation and the normalized dissi- pated energy were 47% and 60% that of the non-corroded control col- umn C0–25, respectively. A comparison among columns with axial load ratio of 0.4 was also made to investigate the inﬂuence of corro- sion level on energy dissipation behavior of specimens. For moder- ately corroded specimen C9–40, the cumulative energy dissipation was 48% that of the control column C0–40, whereas the normalized dissipated energy was 76% that of column C0–40. For severely cor- roded specimen C14–32, the cumulative energy dissipation and the normalized dissipated energy were 30% a

The plots of cumulative energy dissipation ratios of corroded columns and non-corroded control columns Esum/Esum0 versus mass loss ratio q, and normalized dissipated energy ratios of cor- roded columns and non-corroded control columns, kc=kc0, versus q, are provided in Figs. 14 and 15, respectively. The relationship be- tween Esum/Esum0 and q, kc=kc0 and q obtained by linear regression of test data from this study and from Ref. [11] are as follows. Esum ¼ Esum0ð1 /C0 4:789qÞð 12Þ kc ¼ kc0ð1 /C0 3:131qÞð 13Þ The plots of the energy dissipation capacity versus the displace- ment for the specimens with rebar corrosion loss ratio less than 14% are presented in Fig. 16. It should be noted that compared with the non-corrosion control specimens, the energy dissipation of cor- roded specimens at the same displacement excursions is approxi- mately the same regardless of the corrosion level of the columns. The cumulative energy dissipation increases with increasing ulti- mate displacement. However, the energy dissipation of specimen C14–32 was smaller at each cycle after yielding of the longitudinal bars and it averagely decreased 14% compared to the control spec- imen C0–40. 5. Conclusions Thirteen column specimens with different rebar corrosion lev- els and axial loads were tested under cyclic lateral load excursions. Moreover, the relationship between the seismic behavior, such as yield load, ultimate load, average normalized stiffness, displace- ment ductility factor, cumulative energy dissipation, as well as normalized dissipated energy, and rebar corrosion loss ratio was established. The following conclusions can be drawn from this study. 1. Brittle failures were observed in corroded RC columns, in partic- ular in specimens subjected to high axial loads. 2. Higher corrosion level and higher axial load lead to more severely degradation of seismic behavior of reinforced concrete columns. The seriously corroded columns, where corrosion loss ratio is 10–20%, appear to have worse seismic behavior: poor hysteretic response, stiffness degradation, steep descending branch in the envelop curve, and ductility as well as energy dis- sipation reduction. 3. For specimens with corrosion loss ratio less than 14%, the cumulative energy dissipation increases with increasing ulti- mate displacement. Compared with control specimens, the energy dissipation of corroded specimens at the same displace- ment excursions is approximately the same regardless of the corrosion level of the columns.
